one_line_takeaway: DuCodeMark protects code datasets across both completion and decompilation tasks by replacing brittle one-to-one backdoor watermark triggers with a stealthier one-to-style AST/naming-convention scheme, and backstops it with a repressible poisoning mechanism that only degrades model performance (Pass@1 drop of 28.6%) once the watermark is stripped, while remaining far more robust than CoProtector/CodeMark against the DeCoMa watermark remover and other backdoor-defense-derived detectors.
key_finding: Verifiability: DuCodeMark achieves p<0.05 across essentially all 72 settings (e.g., DeepSeek-Coder W1 completion C: p=3.3E-18; decompilation C: p=1.7E-05), while bare (unwatermarked) models consistently give p>0.05 or NaN, and this holds across all 6 temperatures. Harmlessness: watermarking has negligible Pass@1 impact, e.g. SantaCoder C completion 34.45% (bare) vs 33.84% (watermarked) and decompilation 15.24% vs 14.63%. Stealthiness: human suspicion rate is 0.08 (W1) to 0.14 (P) under low-knowledge inspection vs. 0.46 (CoProtector) and 0.14 (CodeMark); under high-knowledge inspection DuCodeMark averages 0.26-0.36 vs 0.68 (CoProtector) and 0.32 (CodeMark). Robustness: against the DeCoMa watermark remover, DuCodeMark's recall (fraction of watermarked samples actually removed) is only 0.34-0.57 versus CoProtector/CodeMark being reduced to non-significance (recall up to 1.00, or p rising above 0.05); against Spectral Signature/Activation Clustering/KillBadCode, DuCodeMark similarly retains significant p-values where the baselines lose verifiability. Watermark survives dilution down to a 50% watermark rate but fails below 20%. Aggressive code obfuscation (Tigress/ProGuard) can strip DuCodeMark's signal (but the paper argues this destroys the dataset's practical value for an attacker); LLM rewriting (GPT-4o) removes it more but at high cost (compilation rate only 0.64, ~925s per 100 samples) and is judged impractical at dataset scale. Poison-activation result: after watermark samples are stripped and the model is fine-tuned only on the leftover poisoned samples, Pass@1 on the targeted return-type functions collapses (e.g., Java completion double-return Pass@1 drops from 32.15% to 3.57% under W1); the paper's headline aggregate figure is a 28.6% Pass@1 drop upon watermark removal, versus only a 0.6% Pass@1 drop from watermarking alone when the watermark remains intact.
core_contribution: DuCodeMark, a code-dataset watermarking method that (1) is the first watermarking scheme to generalize across both source-code tasks (code completion) and code decompilation tasks, (2) replaces prior one-to-one trigger-target backdoor watermarking (used by CoProtector and CodeMark) with a 'one-to-style' scheme in which a fixed trigger (an output-related string literal such as a printf call, or a naming-convention deviation depending on task direction) maps to a target defined by a code style pattern (naming convention or a rare '\n\n' string token) rather than a single fixed token sequence, so as to avoid the frequency-anomaly signature that the watermark-removal tool DeCoMa exploits, and (3) adds a 'repressible poisoning' mechanism: a subset of rare-return-type functions (float/double) is poisoned with semantics-altering transformations, but poisoning is suppressed as long as watermark samples co-occur in the training set and only activates (degrading model performance) once an attacker strips the watermark. Verification is black-box, via an independent-samples t-test comparing model output behavior on triggered vs. non-triggered inputs.
threat_model: Defender = dataset owner protecting a proprietary or license-restricted code corpus (e.g., AnghaBench-style C data, GitHub-derived Java data) who does not know in advance which model or downstream task (code completion vs. decompilation) will consume the data, and who can only modify the raw dataset before release; verification is black-box (API-only access to a suspicious model, no access to weights/architecture). Attacker = someone who steals/repurposes the code dataset to train a CodeLM without authorization; the attacker does not know the trigger/target design or that a watermark is present, but may apply generic defenses: watermark/backdoor detection and removal (DeCoMa, Spectral Signature, Activation Clustering, KillBadCode), automated code formatting (Clang-format), static analysis (CodeQL, Clang Static Analyzer), code obfuscation (Tigress, ProGuard), LLM-based rewriting (CodeLlama-Instruct-7b, GPT-4o), or dataset dilution (mixing in non-watermarked data).
limitations: Evaluated only on C and Java and on relatively small CodeLMs (1B-1.3B parameters, plus one 3B general-purpose check with Llama-3.2-3B); the authors argue but do not empirically verify that effectiveness holds at larger model scales. Watermark verifiability degrades below a 20% watermarking rate under dilution attacks. The method does not leverage comments/documentation, limiting applicability to tasks relying purely on natural-language inputs/outputs (e.g., code summarization, code search) — though the authors show p-values stay far below 0.05 when comments are retained in the C dataset. Aggressive AST-rewriting obfuscators (Tigress, ProGuard) and, more effectively, LLM-based rewriting can remove the watermark, with the tradeoff being reduced dataset utility (obfuscation) or high compute cost and lowered compilation rate (LLM rewriting). The poisoning mechanism as designed targets simple scalar return types (float/double) and the authors note it would be harder to extend safely to complex structured return types (structs/objects) without risking detectable semantic breakage. The paper is self-evaluated against the baselines and attacks the authors themselves selected/adapted; no independent third-party red-teaming is reported.

datasets_used: AnghaBench (C, ~1M compilable C benchmarks, 100,000 sampled after dedup)
Java-decompiler dataset (152,805 compilable Java classes from GitHub via Google BigQuery, 100,000 sampled after dedup)
HumanEval (164 problems, converted to C for evaluation)
HumanEval-X (multilingual HumanEval extension, used for Java evaluation)
quantitative_results: Verifiability p<0.05 across 72/72 experimental settings for watermarked models; bare models p>0.05 or NaN
Harmlessness example (SantaCoder, C): Pass@1 completion 34.45% (bare) vs 33.84% (watermarked); decompilation 15.24% vs 14.63%
Suspicion rate (Group 1, low knowledge): DuCodeMark 0.08 (W1)/0.12 (W2)/0.12 (P) vs CoProtector 0.46 vs CodeMark 0.14
Suspicion rate (Group 2, high knowledge): DuCodeMark avg 0.26 (W1)/0.34 (W2)/0.36 (P) vs CoProtector 0.68 vs CodeMark 0.32
DeCoMa attack recall (code completion, C): CoProtector 1.00, CodeMark 0.98, DuCodeMark-W1 0.35, DuCodeMark-W2 0.55
Dilution attack: watermark verifiable down to 50% watermark rate, fails below 20%
Poison activation example: Java completion, double-return Pass@1 drops from 32.15% to 3.57% (W1) after watermark removal
Headline poisoning effect: Pass@1 drops by 28.6% overall upon watermark removal, vs only 0.6% Pass@1 drop from watermarking alone
Code similarity/compilability after watermarking: W1 similarity 0.98 (C)/0.99 (Java), W2 0.97/0.97, compilability 1.00 in all cases
LLM rewriting attack cost: GPT-4o compilation rate 0.64 post-rewrite, ~925s per 100 samples; CodeLlama-Instruct-7b compilation rate ~0.05-0.06, ~520s per 100 samples
baselines_compared: Activation Clustering (backdoor detection via k-means clustering)
